name: Tengu Lord
description: The Tengu Lord, often referred to as Tengu-sama or Daitengu, is a powerful and complex figure in Japanese mythology. He is not strictly a god, but rather a high-ranking member of the Tengu race, often depicted as a leader or king among them. Tengu are typically portrayed as mountain-dwelling creatures with long noses, wings, and a combination of human and avian features. The Tengu Lord commands respect and fear, embodying both the wild and untamed aspects of nature and the disciplined pursuit of martial arts. He is often associated with ascetic practices, particularly those of the Yamabushi mountain monks, and is seen as a guardian of the mountains and forests. Some Tengu Lords are believed to possess immense magical power, capable of manipulating the elements, shapeshifting, and wielding potent illusions. Their origins are debated, with some theories suggesting they are descended from Garuda, a bird-like deity in Hindu and Buddhist traditions, while others link them to ancient mountain spirits. The Tengu Lord's role varies depending on the specific myth or legend, but he generally acts as a teacher, a trickster, or a protector. He may impart wisdom to worthy individuals, punish those who disrespect nature or abuse their power, or simply create mischief for his own amusement. He is a symbol of both the potential for enlightenment and the dangers of arrogance and pride.
subtitle: King of the Mountain Dwellers

keyMyths.title: The Tengu's Fan
keyMyths.description: A Tengu Lord uses his magical fan to create powerful winds, demonstrating his control over the elements and his unpredictable nature. The fan is a symbol of his power.

keyMyths.title: The Tengu and the Monk
keyMyths.description: A humble monk encounters a Tengu Lord in the mountains and is tested for his worthiness. Only those with pure hearts and strong resolve can earn the Tengu's respect.

keyMyths.title: The Tengu's Teachings
keyMyths.description: A skilled warrior seeks guidance from a Tengu Lord to master the art of swordsmanship. The Tengu imparts secret techniques, but warns against the dangers of arrogance.

sources.author: Donald Philippi
